title: Papel picado tissue cutting
subtitle: Folding and cutting coloured tissue paper into decorative pierced panels
excerpt: Papel picado, Mexican perforated paper, is made by folding a stack of coloured tissue paper and cutting small openings with scissors to produce a lace-like pattern when the sheet is opened out. The cuts are made blind: you do not see the full pattern until the sheet is unfolded. Learning to plan cuts in the folded state is the core skill.
sourceNotes: Papel picado technique synthesised from Mexican craft teaching and general papercutting practice. The tradition is associated with the Day of the Dead celebration but is practised year-round across Mexico and Central America.

glossaryTerms.definition: Decorative perforated paper from Mexico, made by folding and cutting thin tissue paper or foil to produce repeating lace-like patterns when unfolded. Hung in garlands for festivals, celebrations, and altar decoration.

content.text: is a folded and cut technique. The pattern comes from cuts made while the paper is folded, and the full design only shows when the piece is opened out. The fold multiplies every cut, which makes this a good first symmetrical paper-cutting project. The challenge is picturing the reflected result before you cut.

Cut a sheet of coloured tissue paper to a standard rectangle, 28 × 20 cm is a workable size for a first piece. Fold in half lengthwise (folded piece: 14 × 20 cm), then fold in half again across the width (7 × 20 cm). The paper is now four layers. Traditional papel picado is cut through up to 40 layers at once using a chisel and wooden dies, but four layers is manageable with household scissors.

The folded edge is the centre line. Cuts made parallel to and away from the fold create open areas when unfolded. Cuts that cross the fold will open the piece into two separate panels, so all cuts must stop before reaching the fold. In the folded state, plan a series of V-cuts, semicircle cuts, and rectangular notches along the three open edges. Leave at least 5 mm of paper between each cut.

Cut small shapes from the folded edges and from the body of the folded sheet, keeping the scissors as square to the paper as possible. Tissue paper is fragile, cut in short, controlled strokes rather than long sweeps. Circles and curved cuts work better with the scissor tips rather than the full blade. Remove the cut pieces as you go rather than letting them accumulate between the layers.

Unfold gently, working one fold at a time from the last fold back to the first. Flatten carefully: tissue tears if pulled open quickly. Traditional papel picado is strung on a length of thread across the top edge and hung vertically, so the pattern reads against the light. The back-lit effect through coloured tissue is the piece at its best.
